topics.title: Introduction
topics.pageSubtitle: Every core concept explained with visualizations. From basic mathematics to advanced neural networks.

cards.title: What is Machine Learning?
cards.sub: Teaching computers to learn patterns from data without explicit programming
cards.body: Machine Learning is a subset of artificial intelligence that enables systems to learn and improve from experience. Instead of being explicitly programmed for every task, ML algorithms build mathematical models based on sample data to make predictions or decisions.
cards.analogy: Think of it like teaching a child to recognize animals. Instead of programming rules like "if it has four legs and barks, it's a dog," we show the child thousands of pictures of dogs. Eventually, the child learns the pattern and can identify new dogs they've never seen before.
cards.insight: This guide covers 20 fundamental concepts across 6 progressive phases. Each concept includes visual explanations, analogies, and mathematical foundations.

topics.title: Data Basics
topics.pageSubtitle: A dataset is a table. Each row = one example. Each column = one feature (piece of info).

cards.title: Understanding Datasets
cards.sub: The foundation of all machine learning
cards.analogy: Think of it like an Excel sheet. Each student = 1 row. Their marks, age, city = columns (features). The "Label" column is what we want to predict.
cards.insight: Rule: Features → Input to model. Label → What model must predict.

topics.title: Linear Relationships
topics.pageSubtitle: The simplest pattern in data — a straight line relationship between two variables.

cards.title: What is a Linear Relationship?
cards.sub: When one variable changes, the other changes proportionally
cards.body: A linear relationship means that as one variable increases, the other increases (or decreases) at a constant rate. This is the foundation of linear regression and many ML algorithms.
cards.analogy: If you drive at 60 km/h, then: 1 hour = 60km, 2 hours = 120km, 3 hours = 180km. The relationship between time and distance is perfectly linear.
cards.formula: y = mx + b  →  output = (slope × input) + bias
cards.insight: Nearly every ML algorithm starts with this assumption. Even neural networks are stacks of linear + non-linear operations.

topics.title: Vectors
topics.pageSubtitle: A vector is just a list of numbers. In ML, every data point is a vector.

cards.title: Understanding Vectors
cards.sub: The language of machine learning
cards.body: A vector is an ordered list of numbers. In ML, a data point (one row in your dataset) is represented as a vector. Each number in the vector corresponds to one feature.
cards.analogy: A person's profile: [Age=25, Height=175, Weight=70, Score=85]. This is a 4-dimensional vector representing one person. ML models work with millions of such vectors.
cards.insight: Every image, sentence, or data point in machine learning is ultimately represented as a vector of numbers.

topics.title: Linear Regression
topics.pageSubtitle: Predict a continuous number by fitting the best straight line through data.

cards.title: Linear Regression
cards.sub: Predict continuous values with a line of best fit
cards.body: Linear regression finds the line that best fits your data points. It minimizes the total distance between the line and all data points (called residuals). Once trained, you can predict any output by plugging in new input values.
cards.analogy: House prices: given size (sq ft), predict price. You draw the best line through your data, then for any new house size, you read the price from the line.
cards.insight: Used when output is continuous (price, temperature, score). If output is yes/no → use Logistic Regression.

topics.title: Logistic Regression
topics.pageSubtitle: Classify into categories by predicting probability between 0 and 1.

cards.title: Logistic Regression
cards.sub: Binary classification using a sigmoid function
cards.body: Despite the name, logistic regression is used for classification. It squashes the output of a linear function into a probability between 0 and 1 using the sigmoid function. If probability > 0.5, predict class 1.
cards.analogy: Email spam filter: given word counts and sender info, it outputs a probability 0-1. If >0.5 → spam. The sigmoid curve creates an S-shaped decision boundary.
cards.insight: The sigmoid output is a probability. It never goes below 0 or above 1 — perfect for classification.

topics.title: Decision Trees
topics.pageSubtitle: Make decisions by asking a series of yes/no questions about features.

cards.title: Decision Trees
cards.sub: Hierarchical yes/no question structure
cards.body: A decision tree splits data by asking questions about features. At each node, it picks the feature that best separates the classes. It continues splitting until it reaches a leaf node with a prediction.
cards.analogy: Doctors diagnosing: "Fever > 38°C? → Yes: Does patient have cough? → Yes: Flu likely." Each branch is a decision based on a feature value.
cards.insight: Decision trees are interpretable — you can see exactly why a prediction was made. Random Forests combine many trees for better accuracy.

topics.title: Overfitting vs Underfitting
topics.pageSubtitle: The central challenge of ML — finding the balance between too simple and too complex.

cards.title: The Bias-Variance Tradeoff in Practice
cards.sub: Model complexity vs generalization
cards.analogy: Underfitting = memorizing only chapter titles for an exam. Overfitting = memorizing every word but unable to answer paraphrased questions. Ideal = understanding concepts deeply.
cards.insight: Solution to overfitting: more data, dropout, regularization, simpler model.

topics.title: Train/Test Split
topics.pageSubtitle: Always evaluate your model on data it has never seen before.

cards.title: Splitting Your Data
cards.sub: How to properly evaluate model performance
cards.analogy: School exam: you study from the textbook (train set), but the exam questions are new (test set). If your teacher gives you the exam questions to study from — that's data leakage!
cards.insight: Golden rule: Never touch the test set until final evaluation. Use validation set for tuning.

topics.title: Loss Functions
topics.pageSubtitle: How does the model know how wrong it is? The loss function measures the error.

cards.title: What is a Loss Function?
cards.sub: The score of how wrong your model is
cards.body: A loss function computes a single number representing how far the model's predictions are from the actual values. The goal of training is to minimize this number. Different tasks use different loss functions.
cards.analogy: Golf score — the lower, the better. Every training step, the model checks its 'score' and tries to reduce it. Loss = 0 means perfect predictions.
cards.formula: MSE = (1/n) Σ(yᵢ - ŷᵢ)²   |   BCE = -[y log(p) + (1-y) log(1-p)]
cards.insight: Regression uses MSE (Mean Squared Error). Classification uses Cross-Entropy Loss.

topics.title: Gradient Descent
topics.pageSubtitle: The optimization algorithm that trains every neural network.

cards.title: Gradient Descent
cards.sub: Walking downhill to minimize loss
cards.body: Gradient descent iteratively adjusts model weights by computing the gradient (slope) of the loss function. It moves in the direction that decreases loss. Learning rate controls step size.
cards.analogy: You're blindfolded on a mountain. To reach the valley (minimum loss), you feel the slope around you and step in the steepest downhill direction. Repeat until you're at the bottom.
cards.insight: Too large learning rate = overshoot, diverge. Too small = very slow training. Adam optimizer adapts it automatically.

topics.title: Backpropagation
topics.pageSubtitle: How error signals flow backward through a neural network to update weights.

cards.title: Backpropagation
cards.sub: The chain rule applied to neural networks
cards.body: Backpropagation computes gradients layer by layer, starting from the output loss and moving backward using the chain rule of calculus. Each weight receives a gradient telling it how much to change to reduce loss.
cards.analogy: A factory assembly line: a product has a defect. The manager traces backward — was it packing? → assembly? → raw materials? Each step gets a 'blame score' and adjusts its process.
cards.insight: Backprop + Gradient Descent = how all deep learning models are trained. Without it, training a neural network would be impossible.

topics.title: Neural Network Anatomy
topics.pageSubtitle: Layers of interconnected nodes that transform input data into predictions.

cards.title: Anatomy of a Neural Network
cards.sub: Input → Hidden Layers → Output
cards.body: A neural network consists of layers of neurons. The input layer receives data, hidden layers transform it through weighted connections and activations, and the output layer produces the prediction. Each neuron computes a weighted sum of inputs, adds a bias, then applies an activation function.
cards.analogy: Like the human brain — neurons fire signals to connected neurons. Strong connections = high weights. If the signal is strong enough, the neuron activates and passes it forward.
cards.insight: Depth (more layers) lets networks learn complex abstractions. Width (more neurons per layer) increases capacity within each level.

topics.title: Activation Functions
topics.pageSubtitle: Non-linear functions that let neural networks learn complex patterns.

cards.title: Common Activation Functions
cards.sub: Each serves a different purpose
cards.analogy: Without activation functions, a 100-layer network would collapse into a single linear equation. Activations add the 'bends' that let networks fit complex curves.
cards.insight: ReLU is the default for hidden layers. Softmax for multi-class output. Sigmoid for binary output.

topics.title: Dropout
topics.pageSubtitle: Randomly deactivate neurons during training to prevent overfitting.

cards.title: Dropout Regularization
cards.sub: Randomly zero out neurons during training
cards.body: During each training step, dropout randomly sets a fraction of neuron outputs to zero. This prevents neurons from co-adapting too closely, forcing the network to learn more robust, redundant features. Dropout is only applied during training, not inference.
cards.analogy: A sports team where random players sit out each practice. Each player must develop independent skills rather than relying on others. The full team performs better in real games.
cards.insight: Dropout rate of 0.2–0.5 is standard. Higher for larger networks. Always disable during evaluation/inference.

topics.title: Confusion Matrix
topics.pageSubtitle: A table showing true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives.

cards.title: Classification Metrics
cards.sub: Derived from the confusion matrix
cards.analogy: COVID test: True Positive = sick person correctly identified. False Negative = sick person missed (dangerous!). Accuracy alone is misleading when classes are imbalanced.
cards.insight: Use Recall when false negatives are costly (medical). Use Precision when false positives are costly (spam).

topics.title: Bias vs Variance
topics.pageSubtitle: The fundamental tension in machine learning model design.

cards.title: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
cards.sub: Every model lives somewhere on this spectrum
cards.body: Bias = error from wrong assumptions (model too simple). Variance = error from sensitivity to training data (model too complex). As you reduce bias, variance typically increases. The goal is to find the sweet spot.
cards.analogy: Archery: High bias = always hitting left of target (systematic error, bad aim). High variance = shots scattered all over (inconsistent, sensitive). Ideal = tight cluster in the bullseye.
cards.insight: Total Error = Bias² + Variance + Irreducible Noise. You cannot eliminate all three simultaneously.

topics.title: CNNs
topics.pageSubtitle: Specialized neural networks designed to process grid-like data — images, audio, video.

cards.title: CNNs — How Computers See
cards.sub: Convolution layers detect local patterns
cards.body: CNNs use convolutional layers that slide small filters across the input to detect local features like edges, textures, and shapes. Early layers detect simple patterns; deeper layers combine them into complex objects.
cards.analogy: Reading with a magnifying glass: you slide it across the page to read each word (local pattern). Your brain then combines words into sentences into meaning — just like CNN layers.
cards.insight: Conv → ReLU → Pooling is the basic CNN block. Pooling reduces spatial dimensions while retaining the most important information.

topics.title: Attention Mechanism
topics.pageSubtitle: Let the model focus on relevant parts of the input — the foundation of Transformers.

cards.title: Attention & Transformers
cards.sub: Query, Key, Value — the language of attention
cards.body: Attention allows the model to weigh the importance of different input tokens when producing each output token. Self-attention lets each token attend to all other tokens, capturing long-range dependencies that RNNs struggle with.
cards.analogy: Reading a sentence and deciding which words to focus on when translating a specific word. 'The bank by the river' — to translate 'bank', you attend more to 'river' than 'the'.
cards.insight: Attention is the core of GPT, BERT, and all modern LLMs. It replaced RNNs because it parallelizes perfectly.

topics.title: Embeddings
topics.pageSubtitle: Map discrete objects — words, images, users — into continuous vector spaces.

cards.title: What are Embeddings?
cards.sub: Dense vector representations of discrete objects
cards.body: Embeddings convert high-dimensional sparse representations (like one-hot encoded words) into dense, low-dimensional vectors. Similar items cluster nearby in this vector space. The famous example: king - man + woman ≈ queen.
cards.analogy: GPS coordinates for concepts. Cities near each other geographically have similar coordinates. Similarly, semantically related words have similar embedding vectors.
cards.insight: Word2Vec, GloVe, and transformer embeddings power every NLP model in production today.

topics.title: Reinforcement Learning
topics.pageSubtitle: An agent learns by interacting with an environment — reward signals guide learning.

cards.title: Reinforcement Learning
cards.sub: Agent → Action → Environment → Reward → Repeat
cards.body: In RL, an agent takes actions in an environment to maximize cumulative reward. Unlike supervised learning, there are no labeled examples — the agent discovers the optimal strategy through trial and error, guided by delayed reward signals.
cards.analogy: Training a dog: it tries different behaviors (sit, bark, jump). You give treats (reward) for the right behavior. Over time, it learns which actions lead to rewards. AlphaGo and ChatGPT's RLHF work the same way.
cards.insight: RLHF (RL from Human Feedback) is how ChatGPT and Claude were fine-tuned to be helpful and safe.
